critic_raw:
The model comparison here is confounded along multiple dimensions simultaneously: the proposed model (gradient-boosted trees) differs from the baseline (logistic regression) in both algorithm family and feature set (SDOH features are added). This means any observed improvement in AUC-ROC is uninterpretable — it could be due to the GBDT's nonlinear modeling capacity, the additional SDOH features, or the interaction between them. A proper experimental design to test the SDOH hypothesis would hold the algorithm constant (GBDT with clinical features vs. GBDT with clinical + SDOH features), not simultaneously change both the model family and the feature set.

Second, the design uses stratified random splitting on a five-year EHR dataset with multiple admissions per patient. Patient readmission prediction has a known patient-level clustering structure: the same patient may have multiple admissions, and their readmission tendency is partially a stable individual trait. A random split will place different admissions from the same patient across train and test sets, allowing the model to effectively memorize patient-level risk profiles during training and evaluate on the same patients' other admissions. This produces optimistic AUC-ROC estimates that do not represent performance on new, previously unseen patients — which is the operationally relevant scenario.

Third, AUC-ROC as the primary metric for 30-day readmission prediction has been extensively criticized in the clinical ML literature. AUC-ROC measures rank-ordering ability across all thresholds, but clinical decision-making requires a specific operating threshold, and the model must perform well at that threshold. For a 30-day readmission task where the base rate is typically 15-20%, a model can achieve AUC-ROC > 0.8 while having very poor precision at the clinically relevant sensitivity operating point. The design acknowledges precision and recall as secondary metrics but does not specify the operating threshold or target sensitivity level.

Fourth, SDOH features such as income, education, and housing status are known to be incompletely and selectively missing in EHR records. SDOH data availability is correlated with patient demographics, insurance type, and care pathways — meaning missingness is not at random. Imputing SDOH features on training data and evaluating on test data without modeling the missingness mechanism will produce overestimates of SDOH utility if the test set has more complete SDOH data (e.g., because it comes from a period with improved data collection).

Fifth, the design does not address the temporal dimension of the five-year EHR dataset. Hospital policies, coding practices, and readmission drivers change over time (e.g., ACA implementation, COVID-19). A model trained on 2018-2020 data and evaluated on 2022-2023 data faces distribution shift. A random split hides this shift by mixing temporal periods.
all_issues_raised: The model comparison confounds algorithm family with feature set: GBDT with SDOH vs. logistic regression without SDOH changes two things simultaneously, making any observed AUC-ROC difference unattributable to SDOH features specifically.

Patient-level clustering is ignored by the random split: multiple admissions per patient in the same dataset allow the model to learn patient-specific readmission tendencies from one admission and evaluate on another, producing optimistic estimates that do not represent performance on new patients.

AUC-ROC does not capture clinically relevant threshold performance: for 30-day readmission with a 15-20% base rate, high AUC-ROC is compatible with poor precision at the operating sensitivity required for clinical intervention, yet no operating point or target sensitivity is specified.

SDOH missingness is correlated with demographics and care pathways (not missing at random); imputing without a missingness model will overestimate SDOH utility if missingness patterns differ between training and test periods.

The five-year EHR dataset spans major policy changes (ACA, COVID-19); a random split hides temporal distribution shift and will produce overoptimistic generalization estimates compared to a chronological evaluation that reflects deployment conditions.
all_issues_adjudicated: The model comparison confounds algorithm family with feature set: GBDT with SDOH vs. logistic regression without SDOH changes two things simultaneously, making any observed AUC-ROC difference unattributable to SDOH features specifically.

Patient-level clustering is ignored by the random split: multiple admissions per patient in the same dataset allow the model to learn patient-specific readmission tendencies from one admission and evaluate on another, producing optimistic estimates that do not represent performance on new patients.

AUC-ROC does not capture clinically relevant threshold performance: for 30-day readmission with a 15-20% base rate, high AUC-ROC is compatible with poor precision at the operating sensitivity required for clinical intervention, yet no operating point or target sensitivity is specified.

SDOH missingness is correlated with demographics and care pathways (not missing at random); imputing without a missingness model will overestimate SDOH utility if missingness patterns differ between training and test periods.

The five-year EHR dataset spans major policy changes (ACA, COVID-19); a random split hides temporal distribution shift and will produce overoptimistic generalization estimates compared to a chronological evaluation that reflects deployment conditions.
